speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back to-- to-- when-- when you stopped in Bochnia to see your parents--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what was their reaction at seeing you still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Funny, I don't remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the reason I think I don't remember because I didn't want to be involved. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As I told you, I have-- I had to wash them off somehow, they were getting away from me. The attachment is part of this break in a matter that time, they-- like they no longer existed. And I could not hold them in my mind or in my heart, or anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were sort of gone. So if there was any reaction, I don't remember what it was. But as I was pointing out, what has happened, this truck was going to pick up lumber.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so there was-- they was a [? girl ?] very close to Bonchnia that was done by two Jewish drivers from the camp. So they would take people, with permission of the-- of the German construction engineers, were nice, real nice people, basically. So I said I wanted to the see my parents. A number of people during that time would actually escape. And then the Germans would not, at this point, react, [? OK, ?] to the escapes. They really allowed for that. So they-- they dropped me off in Bochnia. And I could spend three or four hours with my parents, just myself. And it was a strange thing. My father was quite sick. He was in the hospital. He was dying. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I came to visit him. But he didn't sort of exist for me. I had nothing to say to him. My mother and my grandmother, they lived in a little place, and they had some of the things we brought from the village, on doilies, or some tablecloths, or something like this, which made me look-- it was like home. It was attractive compared with the way we lived in the camp. I mean the camp was pretty primitive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And grandmother wasn't there. She says, she is someplace gathering wood or whatever. She says we're doing pretty good. We're surviving, she says. She says, can you stay? We would like you to stay. And I said, no. I was terrified of the very idea of staying. And then the truck came back. I don't remember any big goodbyes. I just left. And this was the last time I saw them. So this began for me a pattern of running from a painful feeling, loss. I have already written them off. The loss has taken place. And the facts of our lives, somehow, did not make a difference. I became interested in my own survival. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So this particular-- what I described now between the starting with the day when my mother went to the policeman and my escape, started a-- a new pattern for me, something locked up. I was no-- no longer able to feel or see things the way other people did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, it saved me, it saved everybody who went through the camp. People in the camp-- just as an aside to the story-- after a few days, sometimes, they begin to get numb, emotionally shut down. This is-- the mind actually steps in and cuts the reality so people, instead of being there, become sort of like observed, detached. They no longer feel. The people who did not go through this process, they would die in a matter of weeks, or go crazy, or wind up in the electric wire. So it was a protection. I began to protect myself even before I got to the-- to the-- I mean, labor camp didn't really count, it wasn't so bad. And it has very positive as well as very negative results, this kind of a shutdown. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, this-- I'll come back to this later. But at some point in 1943, in July, they decided to-- to cut down our camp in Rakowice, to reduce to just 20 people left behind, including my brother. And the rest of us moved to Plaszow. This was, I remember, July 3. And commander Goth prepared a show for us, a public hanging the-- to introduce us to the-- to his regime. One of the people who was hung was a young boy, who was a 16-year-old. He sang a [INAUDIBLE], a Ukrainian song. There were Ukrainian guards, in-- in addition to German SS. There were the Ukrainian guards who were working with the Germans. They would often march and sing and he was [INAUDIBLE] on all those catchy little tunes. And this was the reason to hang him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remembered the-- the rope broke loose, and he was begging Goth not to hit him again. And he shot him instead. And the other guy was hung. So this was my introduction to the concentration camp. Now, I was-- I had no skills. I didn't know anybody. I had no connections, which was important in camp, and so on. So I was assigned to what they called [INAUDIBLE], which means barrack building, which was the toughest of jobs, and always exposed to all kinds of dangers, and problems, and shooting, and beating. It was dangerous work. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The strange thing was there was a woman's camp. And I found out that Anita was there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I came back-- when we left Rakowice, I had a little trunk with me. And my brother made me a little jacket from a blanket. But this jacket was like his work of art, beautiful. It was my pride and joy. So in evening after work, I would go to my trunk and put the jacket on and go to see Anita in a woman-- woman's camp, just for a short while.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she had her mother there and-- and the aunt, and still influenced by this Mr. [INAUDIBLE], her uncle, who still had this powerful position. And they were-- they always liked me. The family liked me and all this, and, you know, would give me a piece of bread, or whatever it is. And then I was there maybe a few times. And after work, we're marched to another barrack. I said, well, what about my trunk? They said, forget it, it's gone. I never went to see her again, because I was embarrassed I didn't have my jacket.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had letters from her. She survived. And they were looking for me, I disappeared. It was this kind of embarrassment, the same thing that I washed my knees to see her or I wouldn't go with my dirty work clothes. She, because of the influence of her uncle, wound up on Schindler's list. And she survived through Brunnlitz through the whole thing. Had I been going to see her, there was no question that they would have pulled me over. And not only for [INAUDIBLE] and Schindler, but the position in the camp would have been better for me. So this is another form of running. Strange story, in a way. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- the feelings that you had about Anita, though, were they different than the-- the feelings of detachment that you felt from your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was different. Right. She was something which-- I mean, I was 14. She was 12. I mean there was nothing like a strange feeling. She represented the symbol of something, which I couldn't quite put my finger on. I liked her very much, but it was sort of-- I don't know. The point I'm bringing the story up is that how I could have done better in Plaszow. It was very difficult for me in Plaszow. I wasn't one of those people who were the worst of the worst. For instance, we were in a barrack. And one day-- one night, a bunch of Polish policemen came in. They wanted 50 people. And usually it was-- it was a sacrifice, you got on a-- some form of execution. And they wanted 50 people. This was the place to go to find them. So they're beating us to get dressed, hurry it up. And I was sitting in the bunk, and I was putting my shoes on, tying them slowly. And as soon as I tied them, untied and put them back off again. So I-- I got beaten. But they were very nervous and in a hurry. They got 50 other people before they got to me, because I wasn't ready. This kind of a thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the survival was tough. There was another case in September of '43. I wound up working in-- this story is worth telling. I wound up working in a-- a very crack group, working group, which was construction. Bunch of young people, which is very dynamic leader, our kapo leader. We could do so-- more than anybody else, better job, and quicker than anybody else. We became even the favorites of Goth, who sent us out to do jobs in town, like building a-- a very complex air raid shelter, which he bet some of the other guards there, the Germans, that we could do in one day. There was no way it could be done in one day. We did it. Now, the way it worked, we had barracks throughout the camp. And then in the morning, we used to go to the appellplatz, you know the, the standing grounds. And then after being counted off by the barracks, we would be assigned to the groups of 50, work groups. For the day, we were part of the work group. And the work groups could be made of other people from the different barracks. You understand? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was in a-- summer of '43. There were quite a few escapes. So Goth finally came up with the idea if anybody escapes, the entire group would be shot. And a number of groups were shot, as a matter of fact. Mostly people who worked outside and in town, and came back. Either somebody escaped, or they carried something contraband, or whatever it is. The entire group will be taken up [INAUDIBLE] and be shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. I'll tell you a story about it in a minute. We formed that day, for the-- for work group, and there were 49 of us. One guy is missing. So he escaped. He was in the camp, but somehow managed to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were immediately surrounded by the Polish enterprise, the Jewish police, the [INAUDIBLE]. So we did not run off. But the reporting to Goth would normally be done at 6 o'clock in the afternoon, and then they would be taken out and shot. So we were ready for it, as a matter of fact. The feeling of knowing this kind of really have a death sentence over you, knowing that this is the last time you're going to do this, the last time you see this. We're working some places, tearing down a barrack. I found a pair of shoes and left them alone because what am I going to use a pair of shoes for? Then they took us down to lunch. And at lunch, they announced that nobody's going back to work, that there's going to be a-- a big gathering at the appellplatz and a hanging. They were going to hang us because of the set the example, shooting was not enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were dismissed back into block groups. So our group was separated, and we went back to the-- our blocks. And then at 2 o'clock, when everybody was going to the appellplatz, we had four layers of bunks. I was lying down on the lower bunk, was just talking to somebody. And then everybody left. I tore open the top board, and I sneaked under the bunk. It was maybe something like six, seven inches of space underneath. And I hid. My rationale was, I don't want to be hanged. On the other hand, I can't get away with it. Eventually, I will be found out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what are they going to do? They can beat me or shoot me, but it won't be a hanging. So I took a chance on it. And this, lying there for hours, waiting, it's another experience. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Nevertheless, eventually they all came back. It turned out that they took our group, isolated them, but then he [INAUDIBLE] the camp-- the-- the head of the camp, the Jewish head of the camp, persuaded Goth that we are too valuable. And instead, they had one of the selections. And they selected several hundred people and shot them instead. So when they came back, they didn't even know that I was missing because they disbanded the group. So that's how I-- another story of survival. Now how did you-- you-- you were hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard them come back in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and then after this, I heard what was going on. I just worked my way out, and I was among them like nobody knew I was missing. So this is-- you see, survival of the camp is always a set of strange coincidences, being in the right place at the right time. And not being in the wrong place at the wrong thing. And I have dozens of stories like this. And each one-- you know, you take-- you have to take the sum total, and not-- you can't fail even once. It has to work right every time. Talking to [INAUDIBLE]. They brought a group of people by bus, I remember. And they were Polish people, men and women, who were supposed to take an outing, that for some reason they were considered-- I don't know. They worked for the Germans, became embarrassed, or whatever. They were going to shoot them. They took them up-- up to the [INAUDIBLE]. And our group was working right outside, and digging some ditches or whatever. Normally, when they shot people over there, they had to get undressed, put the clothing in a pile. And then the one guy usually, or two, would just sit on the edge of the ravine, like, swinging his feet, and the people would go back, kneel in front of him with the back of the neck. And he would shoot them. Two people pick them up in the pile, and the next guy will go off. And this is how the execution was taken place, one at a time, with a pistol shot in the neck. These people were shot dressed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And somehow we were grabbed, me and friends of mine, to take the clothing down to the-- what do you call it, where they kept the [INAUDIBLE], this is the clothing barrack, in a wheel barrow. So I remember I went down to this ditch. We went down together. The-- the-- the people were undressed, stacked like wood-- like cords of wood, you know, like going across each other, head to feet, and so on. And I remember this was-- there was one of the people I just saw on side of them was a woman. It was always stuck in my mind, this is the first time I saw a naked woman was a bloodied body. Anyway, we went down. But as we were going down, there was the wheelbarrows full of clothing. We went searching through them to see if there's anything there. And we found a piece of white bread with butter. But it was all bloody. So I remember we carefully picked the blood away and had a feast. And we sat-- you know, they're not the big stories, but somehow, some of these things stick. So this is what life was like in Plaszow. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What unfortunately, as great a movie as it was, concentrated too much on the positive aspect, Schindler and the survivor. Did not show what I was hoping would be shown about life on the-- amongst less fortunate ones. It had some of this, but far from enough. At any rate, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to cut the story short, my brother arrived. They closed up the plant later on-- not the plant, the camp in-- in Rakowice. And he immediately wound up in, again, [INAUDIBLE], the master tailor [INAUDIBLE]. So he, again, had his position. I didn't see much. He was in a different barracks. We didn't see each other at all, as far as I know. Then in 1944, they asked for volunteers for assignment in Krakow. Nobody volunteered. This was a bad thing. But things were so bad, I said, we're going to volunteer. Instead, they took us to Zakopane, which is a resort area in Poland, very beautiful. But there were 150 of us assigned to build-- to divert a mountain stream, to build a hydroelectric plant. I mean, this was murder, unbelievable. This kind of trying to pull stones out of the water and there's still snow and cold. And-- and terrible. Anyway, we did three months at Zakopane, from April to July of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in the group doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we came back to Plaszow. At that time, we were issued stripes for the first time, striped uniforms. It was totally an SS concentration camp, under the jurisdiction of the Gestapo, and all this. Because before it was not. So things got changed for the worse. Anyway, I was there for a month. And at that time, I was wound up together in the same barracks with my brother again. And in August, they began to move people out. Apparently the Russians were coming. So they're moving people out west. And in August, they moved many people, including he-- my brother. And-- and we were transported to Mauthausen, in Austria. What has happened-- there was a shortage of-- of rail equipment. Normally they would put 50, 70 people in cattle cars. They put 140. I mean, it was impossible to stand there. I mean, you know, like a crowded subway in New York, or maybe in Tokyo, it's suspended. And this was the summer heat. And we thought, I mean, this was terrible. They kept us there for a day and a half before we finally moved. No water, nothing. Until they began to spray water on the roof of the car. It made us feel-- they're not gonna kill us because they wouldn't do this kind of thing for us to keep us cooler. They did it to kill us. So all of a sudden, the spirits lifted. Then I read in Schindler's book that Schindler arranged that. This was also the scene in the movie. But of course, this was not the intent to preserve us. They moved us to Auschwitz. In Auschwitz, they wouldn't take us in. So they moved on to Mauthausen. Had we wound up in Auschwitz, we would go straight to the gas chambers. But this was a terrible trip. Many people died on the way because of the-- I mean, heat, and the-- the-- the crowding. I mean, this was unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- how did you deal with the-- the bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the just-- bodies were stuck to the corner so they made room for people. Even-- even then, nobody could lay down or sit down. But this was-- once people began to die, it was sort of make more room because the bodies could be stacked in one corner. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We arrived to Mauthausen. That's a story in itself. We were thirsty. The thirst was unbelievable. It was just trying to remember what it feels like. Because after several days on the train, and then still no water. Finally we got to the showers. We didn't know whether they were gas chambers or not, but when water came in, my, that was something. They assigned us to the blocks. So many of us, that they put us in-- people just sitting, like-- like this. And then they would shove other people and form these long rows. And this is how we spent the night. And then they would take us to the famous stone quarry. The quarry at Mauthausen was a granite quarry. It was in a bottom of the big pit. And there were 186 steps cut in the rock. We were there in 1965 for a trip. So the Austrian government had made the steps, cut them down to different sizes in order for the tourists to be able to go. But there were some steps which were as high as to meters high.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two meters high?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And so-- I mean, they cut in the rock. And there was no-- well, [INAUDIBLE] tell you was what they were doing is they marched us through these stone quarries barefoot, on hard granite, I mean, sharp granite. We would go down, and two people would load up a stone on your back. And you had to climb up. There's no way of climbing up on a two meter stone. There was an idea of walking sort of around, so there were some ledges, without dropping the stone. If you dropped the stone, you were dead. People who dropped the stone, there was one-- they had one particular-- on the top [INAUDIBLE] there was a-- a sharp drop. The prisoners gave it the name, the parachuter's wall. What they would do is just push you over. So people were dying like flies. I mean, and there we had to carry the stones, maybe a mile and a half, two miles. And then run back and start over again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was their test of who's gonna survive. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One day, we had a assembly in front of the barrack. And some German kapos came by, I mean kapos with the prisoners, and look people over, marked down the numbers. And the next day they came back and called people by their number. And I was one of them called. I was telling this to my brother, we were together. And then we were separated, were taken out of the camp to another camp-- another barrack, rather. As it turned out there were only young boys, good looking boys they selected. And I remember there were five or six. So the first day we stayed in the barracks, all of us, and-- cleaning the windows or washing, stuff like this, [INAUDIBLE] soft spot. But the next day, they took four [? they ?] left behind and two of us was sent back to the stone quarry. As it turned out, I mean, many of the block leaders and the kapos and so on were homosexuals. So they picked their victims this way. Fortunately, I wasn't-- I didn't meet their standards.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you were sent back? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the stone quarries.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were one of the two that were sent back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm the-- sent back to the rest of the group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how long, this was two weeks or three weeks in the stone quarry work. And then we were fortunately sent to another camp, a sub-camp of Mauthausen, called Melk, also in Austria. And in Melk, this was a labor camp. We were building tunnels and factories inside mountains, and so on. Very hard work. It was a labor camp. A lot of beating, and a lot of-- and starvation, and all kinds of things. But there were no executions. There were no kind of Mauthausen-type deals. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But things were going from bad to worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were able to-- considering that you were only a teenager--

speaker: interviewee
text: 15, 16. In '44 I was 16. I was very strong. And I-- during the time, I actually [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] found that many people who were strong guys, people who were partisans or fighters, came in, they were got-- within six weeks they were dead. I kept going. So my strength held out, fortunately. I could work very hard. I had absolutely no relief, or no soft chaps. I was working the cement work, and digging, and carrying these heavy metals and whatever. So Melk was a-- OK.
